records.ety: [Prov. E., n., a lump on a tree where a branch has been cut off; v., to cut off the twigs and small branches from a tree, of Celtic origin; cf. Gael. snaigh, snaidh, to cut down, to prune, to sharpen, p. p. snaighte, snaidhte, cut off, lopped, Ir. snaigh a hewing, cutting.]

defs: A stump or base of a branch that has been lopped off; a short branch, or a sharp or rough branch; a knot; a protuberance.
quotes: The coat of arms Now on a naked snag in triumph borne.

defs: A tooth projecting beyond the rest; contemptuously, a broken or decayed tooth.
A tree, or a branch of a tree, fixed in the bottom of a river or other navigable water, and rising nearly or quite to the surface, by which boats are sometimes pierced and sunk.
One of the secondary branches of an antler.
quotes: How thy snag teeth stand orderly, Like stakes which strut by the water side.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Snagged (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Snagging (?).]

defs: To cut the snags or branches from, as the stem of a tree; to hew roughly.
To injure or destroy, as a steamboat or other vessel, by a snag, or projecting part of a sunken tree.
